text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Patient: Raymond Okafor
Attending: Medical Oncology
Visit Type: Established patient, oncology follow-up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Okafor is a 58-year-old Nigerian-American male with metastatic sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (stage IV, KRAS G12D mutant, MSS) presenting for a dedicated visit to review the results of repeat comprehensive genomic profiling from his recent hepatic re-biopsy and circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) analysis, and to discuss implications for his ongoing and future management. He is currently on cycle 8 of second-line FOLFIRI plus Cyramza.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Okafor was diagnosed approximately 15 months ago with stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases. His primary tumor was 4.8 cm with 3 of 18 regional lymph nodes positive (pT3 N2a). Initial molecular profiling was notable for KRAS G12D mutation, MSS status, SMAD4 loss, PIK3CA E545K, PTEN deletion, TP53 R248W, and APC loss -- a genomically complex tumor. He completed 12 cycles of first-line FOLFOX6 plus bev with an initial partial response, during which oxali was discontinued after cycle 8 due to grade 3 cumulative peripheral sensory neuropathy; he was transitioned to maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin plus Avastin. He then underwent laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy approximately 7 months after diagnosis for primary tumor resection. He resumed maintenance 5-FU/Avastin post-operatively.

At approximately 10 months, restaging imaging demonstrated disease progression with growth of the known hepatic lesions and emergence of new bilateral pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe 8 mm, left upper lobe 6 mm, right middle lobe 5 mm), subsequently confirmed as FDG-avid on PET/CT. CEA at progression was 62.3 ng/mL. Given KRAS G12D mutation precluding anti-EGFR therapy and MSS status precluding immunotherapy, he was transitioned to second-line FOLFIRI plus ramu, based on the RAISE trial data supporting survival benefit for ramu over placebo in FOLFIRI-treated second-line CRC.

His course on FOLFIRI/ramu has been notable for early grade 3 diarrhea requiring iri dose reduction to 150 mg/m2, and worsening hypertension on ramu requiring addition of hydrochlorothiazide to his existing lisinopril and amlodipine. Restaging after cycle 4 of FOLFIRI/Cyramza (approximately 13 months from diagnosis) showed a partial response with shrinkage of hepatic and pulmonary metastases, and CEA fell to 41.2 ng/mL.

Given the clinical scenario -- a patient with a complex genomic profile who has received multiple lines of therapy with partial response and known KRAS G12D -- approximately 2 weeks ago he underwent CT-guided core needle biopsy of the dominant segment VII hepatic metastasis for repeat molecular profiling and liquid biopsy, performed to evaluate for acquired resistance mechanisms and to identify potential targets for future lines of therapy. The results of that analysis are available and are the primary focus of today's visit.

Today he reports feeling reasonably well. He notes ongoing peripheral neuropathy in his feet bilaterally (stable, grade 2, predominantly numbness and reduced sensation distally; significantly better in his hands compared to the nadir of grade 3 neuropathy during oxali therapy). He denies any new neurological complaints. Fatigue is mild (grade 1-2), manageable, and not limiting his daily activities significantly. He reports mild hair thinning but no complete alopecia. He is monitoring his blood pressure at home; recent readings 134-140/84-90 mmHg, which he describes as reasonably controlled. No recurrence of diarrhea since iri dose reduction; bowel habits are currently 2-3 loose stools per day (grade 1), manageable with occasional loperamide use. No nausea, vomiting, mucositis, or hand-foot syndrome. No new symptoms referable to his lung lesions -- occasional mild dyspnea on exertion with brisk walking, but no worsening compared to last visit. No hemoptysis, pleuritic chest pain, or new respiratory complaints. He denies abdominal pain, rectal bleeding, or symptoms at the anastomotic site. No unintentional weight loss.

He was accompanied today by his wife. He remains highly motivated to pursue every available treatment option and has expressed strong interest in clinical trials, particularly given his awareness that KRAS G12D was historically considered undruggable. He was recently referred to our early-phase clinical trials team and is in pre-screening for a potential phase II study involving a KRAS G12D-specific inhibitor.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Mild fatigue, grade 1-2. No fever, chills, or night sweats. Weight stable (approximately 84 kg, down approximately 4 kg from baseline 86.4 kg at diagnosis but stable over the past several months).
Neurologic: Bilateral lower extremity peripheral sensory neuropathy, stable at grade 2. Improved hand neuropathy from prior grade 3. Gabapentin providing partial relief.
Cardiovascular: Hypertension managed on triple antihypertensive therapy, BP readings improving. No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema.
Respiratory: Mild exertional dyspnea, unchanged. No cough, hemoptysis, or pleurisy.
Gastrointestinal: Grade 1 diarrhea (1-2 loose stools above baseline per day). No nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, or rectal bleeding. No symptoms at prior anastomotic site.
Genitourinary: No hematuria, dysuria, or proteinuria complaints (urinalysis checked at last visit -- trace protein, attributed to Cyramza-related proteinuria, 24-hour urine protein pending).
Musculoskeletal: Mild joint aches, grade 1, non-limiting.
Dermatologic: Grade 1-2 alopecia. No rash, hand-foot syndrome, or skin toxicity.
Endocrine/metabolic: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, well-controlled on metformin. Recent HbA1c 6.9% (checked last month).
Psychosocial: Patient endorses anxiety regarding the new molecular findings and future treatment trajectory; discussed openly. Wife present and engaged. Social support described as strong.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Metastatic sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, stage IV (M1b, hepatic and pulmonary metastases) -- as detailed above
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- well-controlled on metformin; HbA1c 6.9%
3. Hypertension -- on triple antihypertensive therapy (lisinopril, amlodipine, HCTZ); complicated by bev- and Cyramza-related hypertension
4. Hyperlipidemia -- on statin (atorvastatin)
5. Eloxatin-induced peripheral sensory neuropathy, grade 2 (improved from grade 3)
6. Laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis (approximately 7 months prior)
7. Subcutaneous implanted venous access port, right chest wall (approximately 14 months prior)

SOCIAL HISTORY
Mr. Okafor is a Nigerian-American male who works as a high school principal; currently working part-time in an administrative capacity, having reduced his schedule since initiating second-line therapy. He is married with three adult children. Former smoker with a 15 pack-year history, quit approximately 12 years ago. Occasional social alcohol use, now minimal during active chemotherapy. Denies illicit drug use. Lives with his wife in a two-story home; has adapted home environment for safety given neuropathy (grab bars, bath mat, reduced clutter). He is active in his church community, which he describes as an important source of support.

FAMILY HISTORY
No family history of colorectal cancer. No known hereditary cancer syndrome in the family. Father died of cardiovascular disease. Mother alive with type 2 diabetes.

ALLERGIES
NKDA (no known drug allergies).

MEDICATIONS
1. Camptosar 150 mg/m2 IV every 14 days (dose-reduced from 180 mg/m2 due to prior grade 3 diarrhea)
2. Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1 every 14 days
3. Adrucil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1, then 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours every 14 days
4. ramu 8 mg/kg IV day 1 every 14 days
5. Metformin 1000 mg PO twice daily
6. Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily
7. Amlodipine 10 mg PO daily
8. Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg PO daily
9. Atorvastatin 40 mg PO daily
10. Gabapentin 300 mg PO three times daily
11. Loperamide 2 mg PO as needed (up to 8 mg/day per diarrhea protocol)
12. Ondansetron 8 mg PO as needed for nausea
13. Multivitamin PO daily

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: Well-appearing male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Appears well-nourished, mildly thin compared to baseline. Dressed professionally.
Vital signs: Blood pressure 136/86 mmHg (right arm, seated), heart rate 74 beats per minute, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, temperature 36.8°C, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Weight 84.1 kg (stable from last visit 83.8 kg). BMI 27.3 kg/m2.
ECOG Performance Status: 1.
Head, eyes, ears, nose, throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Mild diffuse alopecia, scalp without lesions. Anicteric sclerae. Oral mucosa moist, no mucositis, no oral lesions.
Neck: Supple, no lymphadenopathy. No JVD.
Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, crackles, or pleural rub. Chest expansion symmetric.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema. Right chest wall: port site well-healed, no erythema, tenderness, or signs of infection. Port accessed without difficulty for today's labs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Well-healed laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy incisions (3 port sites and Pfannenstiel-type extraction site), no signs of hernia or wound complications. No palpable hepatomegaly -- liver span estimated 11 cm to percussion, no tenderness over the right upper quadrant. No splenomegaly. No ascites by exam. Bowel sounds present and normoactive.
Extremities: No edema. No clubbing. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.
Neurologic: Alert and cognitively intact. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 throughout upper and lower extremities. Sensory: reduced light touch and vibration sensation in both feet to mid-shin level bilaterally; sensation in hands intact with only minimal residual loss of fine discriminatory touch in fingertips bilaterally (markedly improved from grade 3 nadir). Romberg mildly positive (mild instability). Gait steady with slight wide base for balance compensation; ambulating without assistive device today.
Skin: Mild diffuse alopecia. No rash, palmar/plantar erythema, or skin changes.

---

LAB RESULTS
CBC: WBC 5.8 x10^9/L (ANC 3.2 x10^9/L, adequate), Hgb 11.4 g/dL (mild anemia, stable), Plt 241 x10^9/L.
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L, Potassium 4.0 mEq/L, Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL (eGFR >60), BUN 14 mg/dL, Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, AST 28 U/L, ALT 32 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 94 U/L (normal). Albumin 3.8 g/dL.
Tumor markers: CEA 47.4 ng/mL (down from 62.3 ng/mL at progression; remains elevated but declining on second-line therapy; slight uptick from 41.2 ng/mL at last formal restaging -- will monitor closely), CA 19-9 109 U/mL (down from 148 at progression, slight uptick from 102 at last restaging).
HbA1c (last month): 6.9% -- well-controlled.
Urine protein/creatinine ratio (today): 0.31 (mildly elevated, below 0.5; monitoring for Cyramza-associated proteinuria; will recheck next cycle).
Lipase: 28 U/L (normal).

---

IMAGING RESULTS
Most recent restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (performed approximately 2 weeks ago at cycle 6 of FOLFIRI/Cyramza): Hepatic metastases remain visible in segments II, IVa, VI, and VII, with the dominant segment VII lesion measuring approximately 2.0 cm -- notably down from 3.2 cm at initial staging and from 2.2 cm at progression. Pulmonary nodules in the right lower lobe (7 mm), left upper lobe (5-6 mm), and right middle lobe (5 mm) appear overall stable to very slightly improved compared to prior. No new sites of disease. Overall the hepatic and pulmonary burden remains in partial response territory compared to the pre-second-line baseline. The re-biopsy of segment VII was performed under CT guidance concurrently with this imaging period.

---

MOLECULAR PROFILING RESULTS -- PRIMARY PURPOSE OF TODAY'S VISIT

Repeat CGP (hepatic segment VII core biopsy, cycle 6 of FOLFIRI/ramu, approximately 14-15 months from diagnosis):

Persistent variants from prior profiling (as expected):
- KRAS p.G12D (VAF 48%, slightly higher than prior 42% -- consistent with tumor cellularity variation or mild clonal expansion)
- TP53 p.R248W (VAF 53%), APC p.R1450* (VAF 46%)
- SMAD4 p.R361H (VAF 21%) -- prognostic marker of poor outcome; persistent
- PIK3CA p.E545K (VAF 22%), FBXW7 p.R465H (VAF 20%)
- PTEN homozygous deletion (exons 1-9) -- dual PI3K/PTEN pathway activation persistent
- CDK8 amplification (slight copy number increase to 5 copies from 4), MYC amplification (copy number 8, up from 6 at baseline)

KEY NEW FINDING:
- ERBB2 (HER2) amplification detected at high level (copy number 8 by CGP). This was NOT present on initial profiling (copy number 2, not amplified), confirming this is an ACQUIRED alteration. This finding is present on concurrent ctDNA (plasma): HER2 amplification copy number 6 in plasma, suggesting meaningful circulating shedding of this clone. KRAS G12D (VAF 1.8%) and TP53 R248W (VAF 2.1%) also confirmed in plasma.

No secondary RAS or RAF mutations acquired. No BRAF V600E. No new actionable fusions. TMB 9 mut/Mb (low). MSS confirmed.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

IMPRESSION:
Mr. Okafor is a 58-year-old man with metastatic MSS, KRAS G12D-mutant sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma who has had a reasonably favorable trajectory -- currently in partial response on second-line FOLFIRI plus ramu. Today's visit is focused on the important and clinically significant finding of acquired HER2 (ERBB2) amplification identified on repeat tissue biopsy and confirmed on liquid biopsy, representing clonal evolution under selective treatment pressure.

1. ACQUIRED HER2 AMPLIFICATION -- DISCUSSION AND PLAN
I spent considerable time today explaining this finding to Mr. Okafor and his wife. I framed this in both a cautionary and potentially optimistic light:

What this means: HER2 amplification in colorectal cancer, when acquired during treatment, is recognized as a potential mechanism of treatment resistance. It leads to overexpression of the HER2 receptor (the same protein targeted by Herceptin and other anti-HER2 therapies in breast and gastric cancers), which can activate downstream survival signaling pathways, partially circumventing the VEGF blockade from ramu and the cytotoxic effects of the Camptosar/5-FU backbone. Importantly, this is not present in the germline, confirming this is a somatic acquired event, not an inherited predisposition.

Current situation -- this does NOT change his immediate treatment: Critically, Mr. Okafor's disease is NOT progressing at this time. He remains in partial response. Therefore, we are NOT switching therapy today. We will continue FOLFIRI plus Cyramza while the disease is controlled, with close surveillance.

Future planning -- HER2-directed therapy: When his disease eventually progresses on FOLFIRI/Cyramza, HER2 amplification opens a meaningful therapeutic avenue. In HER2-amplified (HER2-positive) colorectal cancers, anti-HER2 combinations have demonstrated activity:
- Herceptin (Herceptin) plus Perjeta (Perjeta), per the HERACLES and MyPathway data in HER2+ mCRC
- T-DXd (Enhertu; T-DXd), which has shown impressive response rates in HER2-positive solid tumors including colorectal cancer in the DESTINY-CRC trials, with responses even in KRAS-mutant HER2-amplified CRC in later lines (though data are emerging)
- tuca-based combinations (e.g., MOUNTAINEER data with tuca plus Herceptin)

I counseled him that KRAS mutation has historically been associated with somewhat lower response rates to HER2-directed therapies in CRC compared to KRAS wild-type, but the data continue to mature. T-DXd and tuca-based combinations may have activity even in KRAS-mutant HER2-amplified tumors. This will be a key option at next progression.

KRAS G12D-directed therapy: I reiterated that sotorasib (Lumakras) and adagrasib (Krazati) have established activity in KRAS G12C-mutant cancers (lung, colorectal) but our patient has KRAS G12D, a distinct mutation. KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors (MRTX1133 and others) are in early-phase trials with promising preclinical and emerging clinical data. His referral to our early-phase clinical trials team is active; they are pre-screening him for a phase II study of a KRAS G12D inhibitor. I encouraged him to follow up with that team.

2. ACTIVE TREATMENT -- FOLFIRI PLUS Cyramza, CYCLE 8 DOSING
He will proceed with cycle 9 of FOLFIRI plus Cyramza today.
- iri: 150 mg/m2 (dose-reduced, stable at this dose since cycle 3)
- 5-FU/leucovorin: standard doses
- ramu: 8 mg/kg
Antiemetic premedication per standard protocol (ondansetron, dexamethasone, aprepitant). Atropine available for cholinergic symptoms. Continue loperamide protocol.

Note on CEA/CA 19-9: There has been a mild uptick in CEA (47.4 from 41.2 at nadir) and CA 19-9 (109 from 102) -- while not alarming in isolation, this will be watched closely. These modest rises do not drive a change in therapy at this point given imaging stability, but will factor into next restaging assessment. Next restaging CT is planned after 2 more cycles (in approximately 4 weeks).

3. HYPERTENSION
Blood pressure 136/86 mmHg today -- well within goal range on triple antihypertensive therapy (lisinopril 20 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg). Continue current regimen unchanged. Will continue home BP monitoring with readings logged. Remind patient to call if readings consistently above 150/95 mmHg.

4. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY
Grade 2 sensory neuropathy, stable and improved from prior grade 3 nadir on oxali. Gabapentin 300 mg TID continued. Continue with neurology follow-up. Discuss with neurology whether there is any additional pharmacologic or physical therapy intervention to optimize function. Reassure patient that neuropathy is not expected to worsen further on Camptosar-based therapy.

5. PROTEINURIA MONITORING (ramu)
Urine protein/creatinine ratio 0.31 today -- mildly elevated but below 0.5 threshold for dose modification. Will recheck with next labs (in 2 weeks). If >3 g/24h or persistent grade 2+ proteinuria, will need to consider ramu hold per protocol.

6. DIABETES
HbA1c 6.9% -- excellent glycemic control. Continue metformin 1000 mg twice daily. Continue to monitor fingerstick glucose; patient reports readings generally 110-140 fasting.

7. CLINICAL TRIALS REFERRAL
Active referral to our early-phase team for KRAS G12D inhibitor trial screening. Patient is highly motivated and ECOG PS 1 -- a good candidate if he meets eligibility criteria. Will also alert the team to the newly identified HER2 amplification in case this opens additional trial options. I will personally reach out to my colleagues in the trials unit to expedite his evaluation.

8. PSYCHOSOCIAL
Mr. Okafor and his wife asked many thoughtful questions today. He expressed a range of emotions -- some anxiety about the acquired HER2 finding and what it means for his prognosis, but also hopefulness that it opens a therapeutic option. I validated both responses. I reminded him that his disease is currently controlled, he continues to function well, and the oncologic landscape is evolving rapidly, particularly for HER2-amplified CRC and KRAS G12D-targeted therapy. Referral to oncology social work offered; patient deferred but will consider.

RETURN TO CLINIC:
In 2 weeks for cycle 9 infusion, labs, and toxicity check. Formal restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis planned in approximately 4 weeks.

---

Electronically signed by Attending Medical Oncologist
